etymology.description: semantic 宀 (mián, "roof") + phonetic 龍 (lóng, "dragon") -> favor
etymology.interpretation: The character 寵 (chǒng) combines the semantic component 宀 (mián, "roof"), suggesting the home or a nurturing space, with the phonetic component 龍 (lóng, "dragon"), which contributes the pronunciation. The combination suggests the idea of conferring favor or special care, akin to how one might cherish something precious within one's own home.
etymology.simplification: 寵(寵) (chǒng, "favor") was simplified by replacing the complex 龍 (lóng, "dragon") with the simpler component 宀 and removing multiple strokes, creating 宠(寵).

examples.chinese: 他很宠他的狗(他很寵他的狗)。
examples.pinyin: Tā hěn chǒng tā de gǒu.
examples.english: He pampers his dog a lot.

examples.chinese: 这个孩子被父母宠坏了(這個孩子被父母寵壞了)。
examples.pinyin: Zhège háizi bèi fùmǔ chǒng huài le.
examples.english: This child has been spoiled by the parents.

examples.chinese: 她一直是个备受宠爱的公主(她一直是個備受寵愛的公主)。
examples.pinyin: Tā yīzhí shì gè bèi shòu chǒng'ài de gōngzhǔ.
examples.english: She has always been a much-pampered princess.
